mn140:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā*, while walking on tour among the Magadhans, arrived at Rājagaha;
mn140:1.3: he approached Bhaggava the potter; having approached, he said this to Bhaggava the potter:
mn140:10.11: what was said was in reference to this.
mn140:10.2: so it was said. And in reference to what was this said?
mn140:10.3: Having seen form [*rūpaṁ*] with the eye, one explores form [*rūpaṁ*] that is a basis for joy, explores form [*rūpaṁ*] that is a basis for sorrow, and explores form [*rūpaṁ*] that is a basis for equanimity [*upekkhā*];
mn140:10.4: having heard a sound with the ear …
mn140:10.5: having smelled an odor with the nose …
mn140:10.6: having tasted a flavor with the tongue …
mn140:10.7: having touched a tangible with the body …
mn140:10.8: having known a *dhamma* with the mind, one explores a *dhamma* that is a basis for joy, explores a *dhamma* that is a basis for sorrow, and explores a *dhamma* that is a basis for equanimity [*upekkhā*]—
mn140:10.9: thus there are six explorations of joy, six explorations of sorrow, and six explorations of equanimity [*upekkhā*].
mn140:11.2: so it was said. And in reference to what was this said?
mn140:11.3: The foundation of discernment [*paññādhiṭṭhāno*], the foundation of truth, the foundation of relinquishment, and the foundation of stilling.
mn140:11.5: what was said was in reference to this.
mn140:12.1: ‘One should not neglect discernment [*paññaṁ*], should guard truth, should cultivate relinquishment, and should train only for peace’—
mn140:12.2: so it was said. And in reference to what was this said?
mn140:13.1: And how, *bhikkhu*, does one not neglect discernment [*paññaṁ*]?
mn140:13.3: the earth element, the water element, the fire element, the air element, the space element, and the *viññāṇa* element.
mn140:14.1: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the earth element?
mn140:14.2: The earth element may be internal or external.
mn140:14.3: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the internal earth element?
mn140:14.4: Whatever internally, belonging to oneself, is hard, solid, and acquired, namely:
mn140:14.5: head hair, body hair, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, pleura, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces, or whatever else internally, belonging to oneself, is hard, solid, and acquired—
mn140:14.6: this is called the internal earth element, *bhikkhu*.
mn140:14.7: Both the internal earth element and the external earth element are simply the earth element.
mn140:14.8: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’—thus this should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*].
mn140:14.9: Seeing this thus as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*], one becomes disenchanted with the earth element; one makes the mind [*cittaṁ*] dispassionate toward the earth element.
mn140:15.1: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the water element?
mn140:15.2: The water element may be internal or external.
mn140:15.3: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the internal water element?
mn140:15.4: Whatever internally, belonging to oneself, is water, watery, and acquired, namely:
mn140:15.5: bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, saliva, mucus, synovial fluid, urine, or whatever else internally, belonging to oneself, is water, watery, and acquired—
mn140:15.6: this is called the internal water element, *bhikkhu*.
mn140:15.7: Both the internal water element and the external water element are simply the water element.
mn140:15.8: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’—thus this should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*].
mn140:15.9: Seeing this thus as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*], one becomes disenchanted with the water element; one makes the mind [*cittaṁ*] dispassionate toward the water element.
mn140:16.1: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the fire element?
mn140:16.2: The fire element may be internal or external.
mn140:16.3: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the internal fire element?
mn140:16.4: Whatever internally, belonging to oneself, is fire, fiery, and acquired, namely:
mn140:16.5: that by which one is warmed, that by which one ages, that by which one burns up, that by which what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted is properly digested, or whatever else internally, belonging to oneself, is fire, fiery, and acquired—
mn140:16.6: this is called the internal fire element, *bhikkhu*.
mn140:16.7: Both the internal fire element and the external fire element are simply the fire element.
mn140:16.8: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’—thus this should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*].
mn140:16.9: Seeing this thus as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*], one becomes disenchanted with the fire element; one makes the mind [*cittaṁ*] dispassionate toward the fire element.
mn140:17.1: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the air element?
mn140:17.2: The air element may be internal or external.
mn140:17.3: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the internal air element?
mn140:17.4: Whatever internally, belonging to oneself, is air, airy, and acquired, namely:
mn140:17.5: up-going winds, down-going winds, winds in the belly, winds in the bowels, winds coursing through the limbs, in-breath and out-breath, or whatever else internally, belonging to oneself, is air, airy, and acquired—
mn140:17.6: this is called the internal air element, *bhikkhu*.
mn140:17.7: Both the internal air element and the external air element are simply the air element.
mn140:17.8: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’—thus this should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*].
mn140:17.9: Seeing this thus as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*], one becomes disenchanted with the air element; one makes the mind [*cittaṁ*] dispassionate toward the air element.
mn140:18.1: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the space element?
mn140:18.2: The space element may be internal or external.
mn140:18.3: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the internal space element?
mn140:18.4: Whatever internally, belonging to oneself, is space, spacious, and acquired, namely:
mn140:18.5: the ear holes, the nostril holes, the mouth opening, that by which what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted is swallowed, where what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted stays, and that by which what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted exits below, or whatever else internally, belonging to oneself, is space, spacious, hollow, hollowed, open, opened, not contacted by flesh and blood, and acquired—
mn140:18.6: this is called the internal space element, *bhikkhu*.
mn140:18.7: Both the internal space element and the external space element are simply the space element.
mn140:18.8: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’—thus this should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*].
mn140:18.9: Seeing this thus as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*], one becomes disenchanted with the space element; one makes the mind [*cittaṁ*] dispassionate toward the space element.
mn140:19.1: Then only *viññāṇa* remains, purified and bright.
mn140:19.10: Dependent on contact to be felt as neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*, *bhikkhu*, a neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.11: Experiencing a neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*, one understands: ‘I experience a neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*.’
mn140:19.12: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact to be felt as neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*, the corresponding felt experience—the neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā* arisen dependent on contact to be felt as neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*—ceases and is stilled.’
mn140:19.13: Just as, *bhikkhu*, from the rubbing together and meeting of two sticks heat is born and fire is produced, and from the separation and removal of those same two sticks the corresponding heat ceases and is stilled;
mn140:19.14: so too, *bhikkhu*, dependent on contact to be felt as *sukha*, a *sukha vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.15: Experiencing a *sukha vedanā*, one understands: ‘I experience a *sukha vedanā*.’
mn140:19.16: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact to be felt as *sukha*, the corresponding felt experience—the *sukha vedanā* arisen dependent on contact to be felt as *sukha*—ceases and is stilled.’
mn140:19.17: Dependent on contact to be felt as *dukkha*, *bhikkhu*, a *dukkha vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.18: Experiencing a *dukkha vedanā*, one understands: ‘I experience a *dukkha vedanā*.’
mn140:19.19: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact to be felt as *dukkha*, the corresponding felt experience—the *dukkha vedanā* arisen dependent on contact to be felt as *dukkha*—ceases and is stilled.’
mn140:19.2: And what does one know with that *viññāṇa*?
mn140:19.20: Dependent on contact to be felt as neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*, *bhikkhu*, a neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.21: Experiencing a neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*, one understands: ‘I experience a neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*.’
mn140:19.22: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact to be felt as neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*, the corresponding felt experience—the neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā* arisen dependent on contact to be felt as neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*—ceases and is stilled.’
mn140:19.3: One knows, ‘*sukha*’; one knows, ‘*dukkha*’; one knows, ‘neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*.’
mn140:19.4: Dependent on contact to be felt as *sukha*, *bhikkhu*, a *sukha vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.5: Experiencing a *sukha vedanā*, one understands: ‘I experience a *sukha vedanā*.’
mn140:19.6: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact to be felt as *sukha*, the corresponding felt experience—the *sukha vedanā* arisen dependent on contact to be felt as *sukha*—ceases and is stilled.’
mn140:19.7: Dependent on contact to be felt as *dukkha*, *bhikkhu*, a *dukkha vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.8: Experiencing a *dukkha vedanā*, one understands: ‘I experience a *dukkha vedanā*.’
mn140:19.9: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact to be felt as *dukkha*, the corresponding felt experience—the *dukkha vedanā* arisen dependent on contact to be felt as *dukkha*—ceases and is stilled.’
mn140:2.1: “If it is not troublesome for you, Bhaggava, may we stay one night in the workshop?”
mn140:2.3: But there is a renunciant here who has already come to stay first.
mn140:2.4: If he permits it, stay, *bhante*, as you wish.”
mn140:20.1: Then only equanimity [*upekkhā*] remains, purified, bright, pliant, workable, and luminous.
mn140:20.2: Just as, *bhikkhu*, a skilled goldsmith or goldsmith’s apprentice might prepare a furnace, prepare the furnace, light the furnace opening, take gold with tongs and put it into the furnace opening; from time to time he would blow on it, from time to time sprinkle it with water, and from time to time look on at it; then that gold would be well refined, thoroughly refined, drawn out, cleansed of dross, pliant, workable, and luminous, and whatever kind of ornament he wished it for—whether a plate, earrings, a necklace, or a gold garland—it would serve that purpose for him;
mn140:20.3: so too, *bhikkhu*, then only equanimity [*upekkhā*] remains, purified, bright, pliant, workable, and luminous.
mn140:21.2: ‘If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhaṁ*], so purified and so bright, toward the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*], and develop the mind [*cittaṁ*] in accordance with that,
mn140:21.3: this equanimity [*upekkhā*] of mine, dependent on that and taking that as *upādāna*, would stand for a long, long time.
mn140:21.4: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhaṁ*], so purified and so bright, toward the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], and develop the mind [*cittaṁ*] in accordance with that,
mn140:21.5: this equanimity [*upekkhā*] of mine, dependent on that and taking that as *upādāna*, would stand for a long, long time.
mn140:21.6: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhaṁ*], so purified and so bright, toward the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], and develop the mind [*cittaṁ*] in accordance with that,
mn140:21.7: this equanimity [*upekkhā*] of mine, dependent on that and taking that as *upādāna*, would stand for a long, long time.
mn140:21.8: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhaṁ*], so purified and so bright, toward the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*], and develop the mind [*cittaṁ*] in accordance with that,
mn140:21.9: this equanimity [*upekkhā*] of mine, dependent on that and taking that as *upādāna*, would stand for a long, long time.’
mn140:22.10: One neither fabricates that nor forms intention toward *bhava* or non-*bhava*.
mn140:22.11: Not fabricating, not forming intention toward *bhava* or non-*bhava*, one does not take up anything in the world.
mn140:22.12: Not taking up, one is not agitated; not agitated, one personally becomes fully quenched.
mn140:22.13: One understands: ‘Birth is destroyed, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state.’
mn140:22.2: ‘If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhaṁ*], so purified and so bright, toward the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*], and develop the mind [*cittaṁ*] in accordance with that,
mn140:22.4: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhaṁ*], so purified and so bright, toward the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], and develop the mind [*cittaṁ*] in accordance with that,
mn140:22.6: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhaṁ*], so purified and so bright, toward the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], and develop the mind [*cittaṁ*] in accordance with that,
mn140:22.8: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhaṁ*], so purified and so bright, toward the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*], and develop the mind [*cittaṁ*] in accordance with that,
mn140:23.1: If one feels a *sukha vedanā*, one understands: ‘It is impermanent [*aniccā*]’; one understands: ‘It is not clung to’; one understands: ‘It is not delighted in.’
mn140:23.2: If one feels a *dukkha vedanā*, one understands: ‘It is impermanent [*aniccā*]’; one understands: ‘It is not clung to’; one understands: ‘It is not delighted in.’
mn140:23.3: If one feels a neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*, one understands: ‘It is impermanent [*aniccā*]’; one understands: ‘It is not clung to’; one understands: ‘It is not delighted in.’
mn140:24.1: If one feels a *sukha vedanā*, one feels it detached;
mn140:24.2: if one feels a *dukkha vedanā*, one feels it detached;
mn140:24.3: if one feels a neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*, one feels it detached.
mn140:24.4: Experiencing a *vedanā* limited by the body, one understands: ‘I experience a *vedanā* limited by the body’; experiencing a *vedanā* limited by life, one understands: ‘I experience a *vedanā* limited by life’;
mn140:24.5: one understands: ‘With the breakup of the body, after death, following the exhaustion of life, all that is felt, not delighted in, will grow cool right here.’
mn140:24.6: Just as, *bhikkhu*, an oil lamp burns dependent on oil and dependent on a wick;
mn140:24.7: with the exhaustion of that very oil and wick, and with no other supply brought, it goes out without fuel;
mn140:24.8: so too, *bhikkhu*, experiencing a *vedanā* limited by the body, one understands: ‘I experience a *vedanā* limited by the body’; experiencing a *vedanā* limited by life, one understands: ‘I experience a *vedanā* limited by life’;
mn140:24.9: one understands: ‘With the breakup of the body, after death, following the exhaustion of life, all that is felt, not delighted in, will grow cool right here.’
mn140:25.1: Therefore a *bhikkhu* endowed in this way is endowed with this highest foundation of discernment [*paññādhiṭṭhānena*].
mn140:25.2: For this, *bhikkhu*, is the highest noble discernment [*paññā*], that is:
mn140:25.3: knowledge of the destruction of all *dukkha*.
mn140:26.1: That liberation of his, founded on truth, is unshakable.
mn140:26.2: For that is false, *bhikkhu*, which is liable to deceive; that is true, which is not liable to deceive: *nibbāna*.
mn140:26.3: Therefore a *bhikkhu* endowed in this way is endowed with this highest foundation of truth.
mn140:26.4: For this, *bhikkhu*, is the highest noble truth, that is:
mn140:27.1: Formerly, when he was unknowing, he had acquisitions, taken up and assumed.
mn140:27.2: They have been abandoned by him, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made non-existent, and not liable to arise in the future.
mn140:27.3: Therefore a *bhikkhu* endowed in this way is endowed with this highest foundation of relinquishment.
mn140:27.4: For this, *bhikkhu*, is the highest noble relinquishment, that is:
mn140:27.5: the giving up of all acquisitions.
mn140:28.1: Formerly, when he was unknowing, he had covetousness, desire, and lust.
mn140:28.2: It has been abandoned by him, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made non-existent, and not liable to arise in the future.
mn140:28.3: Formerly, when he was unknowing, he had hostility, ill will, and hatred.
mn140:28.4: It has been abandoned by him, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made non-existent, and not liable to arise in the future.
mn140:28.5: Formerly, when he was unknowing, he had blindness [*avijjā*] and delusion.
mn140:28.6: It has been abandoned by him, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made non-existent, and not liable to arise in the future.
mn140:28.7: Therefore a *bhikkhu* endowed in this way is endowed with this highest foundation of stilling.
mn140:28.8: For this, *bhikkhu*, is the highest noble stilling, that is:
mn140:28.9: the stilling of greed, hate, and delusion.
mn140:29.1: ‘One should not neglect discernment [*paññaṁ*], should guard truth, should cultivate relinquishment, and should train only for peace’—
mn140:29.2: what was said was in reference to this.
mn140:3.1: Now at that time a clansman named Pukkusāti had gone forth from the household life into homelessness out of faith, with the *Bhagavā* in mind.
mn140:3.2: He had already come to stay first in that potter’s workshop.
mn140:3.3: Then the *Bhagavā* approached Venerable Pukkusāti; having approached, he said this to Venerable Pukkusāti:
mn140:3.4: “If it is not troublesome for you, *bhikkhu*, may we stay one night in the workshop?”
mn140:3.6: Let the venerable one stay as he wishes.”
mn140:30.1: ‘Standing on which the *āsavas* of conceiving do not proceed; and when the *āsavas* of conceiving do not proceed, the sage is called peaceful’—
mn140:30.2: so it was said. And in reference to what was this said?
mn140:31.1: ‘I am,’ *bhikkhu*, is a conceiving; ‘I am this,’ is a conceiving; ‘I will be,’ is a conceiving; ‘I will not be,’ is a conceiving; ‘I will be possessed of form [*rūpī*],’ is a conceiving; ‘I will be formless,’ is a conceiving; ‘I will be percipient with *saññā*,’ is a conceiving; ‘I will be without *saññā*,’ is a conceiving; ‘I will be neither with *saññā* nor without *saññā*,’ is a conceiving.
mn140:31.2: Conceiving, *bhikkhu*, is a disease; conceiving is a boil; conceiving is a dart.
mn140:31.3: Through the complete going beyond of all conceivings, *bhikkhu*, the sage is called peaceful.
mn140:31.4: And the sage, *bhikkhu*, peaceful, is not born, does not age, does not die, is not shaken, does not long.
mn140:31.5: For there is nothing in him by which he might be born; not being born, how would he age? Not aging, how would he die? Not dying, how would he be shaken? Not being shaken, for what would he long?
mn140:32.1: ‘Standing on which the *āsavas* of conceiving do not proceed; and when the *āsavas* of conceiving do not proceed, the sage is called peaceful’—
mn140:32.2: what was said was in reference to this.
mn140:32.3: This, *bhikkhu*, you should remember as my brief analysis of the six elements.”
mn140:33.2: “My teacher has come to me! The well-gone one has come to me! The perfectly awakened one has come to me!” Rising from his seat, arranging his robe over one shoulder, and bowing with his head at the *Bhagavā*’s feet, he said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn140:33.3: “A transgression has overcome me, *bhante*, as a fool, as one confused, as one unskillful, in that I thought the *Bhagavā* should be addressed with the word ‘friend.’
mn140:33.4: May the *Bhagavā* accept my transgression as a transgression, *bhante*, for restraint in the future.”
mn140:33.5: “Indeed, *bhikkhu*, a transgression overcame you as a fool, as one confused, as one unskillful, in that you thought I should be addressed with the word ‘friend.’
mn140:33.6: But since you see the transgression as a transgression and make amends in accordance with the *dhamma*, we accept it from you.
mn140:33.7: For this is growth in the noble one’s *vinaya*, *bhikkhu*: one who sees a transgression as a transgression, makes amends in accordance with the *dhamma*, and undertakes restraint in the future.”
mn140:34.1: “May I receive the going-up in the *Bhagavā*’s presence, *bhante*.”
mn140:34.2: “But is your bowl and robe complete, *bhikkhu*?”
mn140:34.3: “My bowl and robe are not complete, *bhante*.”
mn140:34.4: “*Tathāgatas*, *bhikkhu*, do not give the going-up to one whose bowl and robe are incomplete.”
mn140:35.1: Then Venerable Pukkusāti, having delighted and rejoiced in the *Bhagavā*’s words, rose from his seat, paid homage to the *Bhagavā*, circumambulated him keeping him on his right, and departed in search of bowl and robe.
mn140:35.2: Then, while Venerable Pukkusāti was wandering in search of bowl and robe, a runaway cow deprived him of life.
mn140:36.1: Then several *bhikkhus* approached the *Bhagavā*; having approached, they paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, those *bhikkhus* said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn140:36.2: “That clansman named Pukkusāti, *bhante*, whom the *Bhagavā* instructed with brief advice, has died.
mn140:36.3: What is his destination? What is his future state?”
mn140:36.4: “Wise, *bhikkhus*, was the clansman Pukkusāti. He practiced in accordance with the *dhamma* and did not trouble me on account of the *dhamma*.
mn140:36.5: The clansman Pukkusāti, *bhikkhus*, with the complete destruction of the five lower fetters, has arisen spontaneously there, will attain final *nibbāna* there, and is by nature not returning from that world.”
mn140:36.7: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn140:36.8: The Analysis of the Elements is finished, the tenth.
mn140:4.1: Then the *Bhagavā* entered the potter’s workshop, spread out a grass mat to one side, and sat down cross-legged, holding his body erect and establishing *sati* before him.
mn140:4.2: Then the *Bhagavā* spent much of the night sitting.
mn140:4.3: Venerable Pukkusāti too spent much of the night sitting.
mn140:4.6: What if I were to question him?”
mn140:4.7: Then the *Bhagavā* said this to Venerable Pukkusāti:
mn140:5.1: “With whom in mind have you gone forth, *bhikkhu*? Or who is your teacher? Or whose *dhamma* do you approve?”
mn140:5.10: That *Bhagavā*, the *arahant*, the perfectly awakened one, is dwelling there now.”
mn140:5.12: and if you saw him, would you recognize him?”
mn140:5.13: “I have not seen that *Bhagavā* before, friend;
mn140:5.14: and if I saw him, I would not recognize him.”
mn140:5.2: “There is, friend, the ascetic Gotama, a Sakyan son gone forth from a Sakyan family.
mn140:5.3: Concerning that *Bhagavā* Gotama such a good report has spread:
mn140:5.4: ‘Thus he is the *Bhagavā*, an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened one, accomplished in true knowledge and conduct, well-gone, knower of the world, unsurpassed trainer of persons to be tamed, teacher of *devas* and humans, awakened, *Bhagavā*.’
mn140:5.5: I have gone forth with that *Bhagavā* in mind.
mn140:5.8: “But where, *bhikkhu*, is that *Bhagavā*, the *arahant*, the perfectly awakened one, dwelling now?”
mn140:5.9: “There is, friend, a city named Sāvatthī in the northern countries.
mn140:6.2: “This clansman has gone forth with me in mind.
mn140:6.3: What if I were to teach him the *dhamma*?”
mn140:6.6: Listen to it, attend carefully; I will speak.”
mn140:6.7: “Yes, friend,” Venerable Pukkusāti replied to the *Bhagavā*.
mn140:7.1: “‘This person, *bhikkhu*, has six elements, six dimensions of contact [*phassāyatano*], eighteen mental explorations, and four foundations;
mn140:7.2: standing on which the *āsavas* of conceiving do not proceed; and when the *āsavas* of conceiving do not proceed, the sage is called peaceful.
mn140:7.3: One should not neglect discernment [*paññaṁ*], should guard truth, should cultivate relinquishment, and should train only for peace’—
mn140:7.4: this is the summary of the analysis of the elements.
mn140:8.2: so it was said. And in reference to what was this said?
mn140:8.4: the earth element, the water element, the fire element, the air element, the space element, and the *viññāṇa* element.
mn140:8.6: what was said was in reference to this.
mn140:9.1: ‘This person, *bhikkhu*, has six dimensions of contact [*phassāyatano*]’—
mn140:9.2: so it was said. And in reference to what was this said?
mn140:9.3: The dimension of eye-contact [*cakkhusamphassāyatanaṁ*], the dimension of ear-contact [*sotasamphassāyatanaṁ*], the dimension of nose-contact [*ghānasamphassāyatanaṁ*], the dimension of tongue-contact [*jivhāsamphassāyatanaṁ*], the dimension of body-contact [*kāyasamphassāyatanaṁ*], and the dimension of mind-contact [*manosamphassāyatanaṁ*].
mn140:9.4: ‘This person, *bhikkhu*, has six dimensions of contact [*phassāyatano*]’—
mn140:9.5: what was said was in reference to this.
